title: L04.4.cf more loops
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making Lecture 4.4: Control Flow - Loops (Part II)

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin UNSW

Control Flow: While Loops  Control Flow: While Loops

While loops are another looping mechanism that enables the execution of a set of statements as long as a specified condition is truthy.

1 while <condition_is_truthy>: 2 while_loop_code_block

The while loop syntax consists of the following: The while keyword to indicate the beginning of a loop. A condition that is evaluated before each iteration of the loop An indented block to execute repeatedly as long as the condition holds.

Control Flow: While Loops

Consider the following example to sum up the integers less than or equal to 100 using a for loop:

1 the_sum = 0 2 for i in range(1, 101): 3 the_sum = the_sum + i 4 print(the_sum)

The same task using a while loop:

1 the_sum = 0 2 i = 1 3 while i <= 100: 4 the_sum = the_sum + i 5 i = i + 1 6 print(the_sum)

Initialising variables before the while loop starts and updating them after each loop execution is a common pattern. We will return to this later.

Control Flow: While Loops – Recommendations

While loops are valuable in certain situations, but they may create infinite loops if not well specified. Ensure the condition will ultimately evaluate to “falsy” to prevent the loop from running indefinitely. For loops are more commonly used in practice due to their ease of control and safety against infinite looping.

Nested loops involve an outer loop and an inner loop, where the inner loop is indented within the outer loop. 1 for outer_var in outer_iterable: 2 # runs once per outer_var 3 stmts_before_inner 5 for inner_var in inner_iterable: 6 # runs once per each inner_var for each outer_var 7 inner_code_block 9 # runs once per outer_var 10 stmts_after_inner

When evaluating nested loops, Python will: First outer iteration:  outer_var is assigned to the first object in outer_iterator  stmts_before_inner are executed. For these statements: • outer_var is available • inner_var is not available  The inner loop is executed over all elements of inner_iterator. In inner_code_block: • outer_var is available • inner_var is available  stmts_after_inner are executed. For these statements: • outer_var is available • inner_var is the last iterated value. It should be used only in special cases. Subsequent outer iterations:  Inner loop is executed over all elements of inner_iterator for each iteration over outer_iterator.

Consider printing out each month from 2018 through 2020 in sequence: 1 years = [2018, 2019, 2020] 2 months = [ 3 "Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun", 4 "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec" 5 ] 7 for year in years: 8 for month in months: 9 print("Year: {}, Month: {}".format(year, month))

In this example, The outer loop iterates over years and the inner loop iterates over months. The inner loop is executed completely for each item in the outer loop.

Nested loops offer a powerful way to iterate through multiple levels of data.

Experiment with loop ordering to understand the changes in execution order. While nested loops are useful, excessive levels of nesting (e.g., three or more levels) can make the code more challenging to maintain and comprehend. For complex nested structures, consider alternative programming techniques, such as functions or classes, for better code organisation.

Control Flow: Loops – continue and break  Control Flow: Loops – continue and break

continue and break statements provide additional control over loops.

The continue statement allows you to skip the rest of the current iteration in a loop and move to the next iteration. The break statement terminates a loop immediately once a condition is met.

Control Flow: Loops – The continue statement

The continue statement allows you to skip the rest of the current iteration in a loop and move to the next iteration.

Example: Adding all even numbers from 1 through 100: 1 sum_of_evens = 0 3 # Loop from 1 to 100 4 # Stopping at `var` + 1 ensures `var` is included in the range. 5 for i in range(1, 101): 6 if i % 2 == 1: # Skip odd numbers 7 continue 8 sum_of_evens += i 10 print(f'Sum of evens is {sum_of_evens}')

Python executes this loop as follows: When i is an odd number:  i % 2 == 1 evaluates to True  The continue statement is triggered, and the Python interpreter skips the remainder of the loop, proceeding immediately to the next iteration. When i is an even number:  i % 2 == 1 evaluates to False  The continue statement is skipped  The number is added to the sum_of_evens accumulator

Control Flow: Loops – The break statement

The break statement terminates a loop immediately once a condition is met.

Example: Finding the first Fibonacci number greater than 10,000: 1 current = 1 2 prior = 0 4 while True: 5 current, prior = current + prior, current 6 if current >= 10000: 7 break 9 print(f'First Fibonacci value greater than 10,000 is {current}')

Python executes this loop as follows: Each Fibonacci value is computed Once current is greater than or equal to 10,000, the if condition holds and the break statement is execute, exiting the loop

Control Flow: Loops – break and continue in nested loops

In nested loops, the break and continue statements pertain to the innermost loop. 1 for i in range(3): 2 for j in range(3): 3 if j == 1: 4 break # Only exits the inner loop 5 print(i, j) 7 # Output 8 # 0 0 9 # 1 0 10 # 2 0

It often necessary to track information as a loop executes from one iteration to the next using loop variables. 1 # Loop initialisation 2 variable = initial_value 4 # Loop 5 for element in sequence: 6 variable = updated_value

A common pattern is to initialise loop variables needed for any such information before the loop and update them as the loop proceeds.

Loop variables track information across iterations as a loop executes.

These variables generally serve one of the following purposes: Loop control:  Governs the loop condition or drives iteration (e.g., index counters) Accumulator:  Aggregates a running total or result (e.g., sums, products, string joins) State:  Carries information from one iteration to the next (e.g., previous values, flags)

A loop control variable helps determine the control flow of the loop.

Common roles include: Counting:  Used to repeat a block of code a specific number of times. Indexing:  Used to access elements of a sequence like a list or array. Condition checking:  Used in loops to determine whether to exit.

A loop control variable helps determine the control flow of the loop.

The pattern: Starts with an initial value before the loop begins. Changes with each iteration of the loop (e.g., increments or decrements). Influences the loop’s condition (when to continue or exit). Can also be used within the loop body for indexing, timing, or tracking progress.

Example: Loan repayment simulation 1 balance = 1000 # Starting debt 2 monthly_payment = 150 3 interest_rate = 0.02 # 2% monthly interest 5 while true: 6 interest = balance * interest_rate 7 balance = balance + interest - monthly_payment 9 print(f"Remaining balance = ${balance:.2f}") 11 if balance <= 0: 12 break 14 print("Debt fully repaid.")

An accumulator variable collects (or accumulates) a result as a loop runs.

Common uses include: Summing numbers (e.g., total income) Counting occurrences Building strings or lists Combining data across time or categories

An accumulator variable collects (or accumulates) a result as a loop runs.

The pattern: Is initialised before the loop (often to 0, [], or ““, depending on the data type). Is updated in each iteration of the loop, creating a running total or combined result by the end of the loop.

Example: Computing total interest paid over a loan’s life: 1 loan_balance = 5000 # initial loan amount 2 monthly_payment = 200 # fixed monthly payment 3 monthly_interest_rate = 0.015 # 1.5% interest per month 4 total_interest_paid = 0 # interest accumulator 5 month = 0 # month counter (accumulator) 7 while loan_balance > 0: 8 month += 1 # add to month 9 interest = loan_balance * monthly_interest_rate 10 principal_payment = monthly_payment - interest 12 # Prevent overpayment in final month 13 if principal_payment > loan_balance: 14 principal_payment = loan_balance 15 monthly_payment = interest + principal_payment 17 loan_balance -= principal_payment 18 total_interest_paid += interest # accumulating interest 21 print(f"Loan repaid in {month} months.")

A state variable stores information about the loop’s condition or status.

The term state variable is used for loop variables that are maintained across iterations but are neither a loop control variable nor a state variable. Example: Tracking loan delinquency based on consecutive missed payments 1 loan_balance = 1000 2 monthly_payment_due = 100 3 missed_payments = 0 # state variable 5 payments_made = [100, 100, 0, 0, 100, 100, 0] 7 for month, payment in enumerate(payments_made, 1): 8 if payment < monthly_payment_due: 9 missed_payments += 1 10 else: 11 missed_payments = 0 # reset if payment is made 13 if missed_payments >= 2: 14 delinquent = True 15 elif missed_payments == 0: 16 delinquent = False 18 print(f"Month {month}: Paid ${payment}, Delinquent? {delinquent}")
